meta.title: PicoMUTX — Get an AI Agent Working and Under Control
meta.description: PicoMUTX helps founders and operators build, recover, and control AI agents they can actually trust in real-world use.

hero.title: Get an AI agent working
hero.titleAccent: and trust it in real work.
hero.subtitle: Start from zero, recover a broken setup, or take control of a live agent. Pico routes you into build, fix, or control.
hero.meta: Request-first outside. Inside, Pico diagnoses the blocker and routes you into build, fix, or control.

items.0: Start from zero, a messy build, or a live system
items.1: One diagnosis layer routes you into build, fix, or control
items.2: Visibility, approvals, and safeguards before trust is assumed

problem.title: Most agent projects do not fail
problem.body: Projects stall when the path gets messy: too many stack decisions, no recovery path, and no operational clarity once something breaks.

0.label: "I know what I want the agent to do. I still need the fastest sane path to get one working."
0.body: Starting from zero is not the real problem. The real problem is choosing the stack, sequence, and standards without turning the work into endless research.

1.label: "I already built part of this. Now I need to recover the setup instead of restarting from scratch."
1.body: When a build breaks halfway through, losing momentum costs more than time. You need a recovery lane, not another theory rabbit hole.

2.label: "Something is already live, but I do not trust the cost, logic, or failure modes yet."
2.body: At that point it is not enough for the agent to exist. It has to be legible, controllable, and safe enough to touch real work.

3.label: "I need a system I can explain, review, and control before I rely on it."
3.body: If you cannot see runs, thresholds, approvals, and behavior under pressure, you do not have an operational asset yet.

problem.close: Pico turns that uncertainty into one honest next move.

platform.title: Pico diagnoses the blocker, then routes you to build, fix, or control.
platform.body: Get something useful working and keep it trustworthy. Pico starts with diagnosis so the next move matches the real blocker.

0.title: Describe the goal or connect the agent
0.body: Start with the job, the blocker, or the live system you need to understand. Pico uses that context to decide what kind of help is actually relevant.

1.title: Get routed into build, fix, or control
1.body: New builds get a practical path to one useful agent. Broken setups get recovery steps. Live systems get visibility, thresholds, approvals, and operator guardrails.

2.title: Apply changes in one operational loop
2.body: Observe behavior, isolate what is off, implement the next change, and keep moving without losing the thread.

3.title: Escalate when the edge case is real
3.body: When the system cannot close the loop alone, human guidance steps in as proof and calibration, not as the entire product.

who.title: PicoMUTX is not for everyone.
who.notForYouTitle: This is not for you if…

forYou.0: You want to ship one useful agent without assembling the whole stack by guesswork
forYou.1: You already have a half-built or broken setup and need to recover momentum
forYou.2: You have something live or nearly live and need more control before you trust it
forYou.3: You want outcome-first help, not a course or theory rabbit hole
forYou.4: You want a system that reduces dependence on ad hoc expert intervention over time

notForYou.0: You want a generic no-code toy that removes judgment from the work
notForYou.1: You already need enterprise-grade governance infrastructure across a mature agent estate
notForYou.2: You want education, hype, or trend-chasing more than operational progress
notForYou.3: You expect AI to replace ownership, review, or accountability
notForYou.4: You are only looking to buy access to a founder instead of using a product

beforeAfter.title: What changes when build, recovery, and control live in one place

0.before: You are not sure whether you need to build, debug, or add controls first
0.after: You get routed to the lane that matches the real blocker

1.before: Every issue sends you into a different tool, doc, or opinion loop
1.after: Build, recovery, and control live inside one operating system

2.before: A broken setup or vague runtime behavior kills momentum
2.after: The next action is visible, reviewable, and grounded

3.before: You have an agent you cannot fully explain or trust
3.after: You have something you can run, inspect, and improve with confidence

beforeAfter.close: The goal is not to learn AI in the abstract. The goal is to get something useful working and keep it trustworthy.

faq.title: Questions worth clearing up before you commit

0.q: Do I need to know how to code?
0.a: No. PicoMUTX is for operators who need outcomes without turning themselves into full-time agent engineers.

1.q: What if I already tried and failed?
1.a: That is one of the main entry points. PicoMUTX is built to recover momentum after the first confusing, expensive, or credibility-killing blocker.

2.q: What kind of agent work is this for?
2.a: Practical agent systems: support flows, internal copilots, lead capture, sales follow-up, and operational workflows that need to get built, fixed, or controlled in the real world.

3.q: Is there a free way to start?
3.a: Yes. The public ladder starts with a free trial focused on first value. Public access is still waitlist-first, but the trial and founding tiers are already defined.

4.a: PicoMUTX is the commercial wedge into MUTX, not the full enterprise governance layer. Heavier multi-team governance belongs in the broader MUTX stack.

studio.label: Studio review board
studio.title: Brief, deliverable, critique
studio.stamp: one real lesson at a time

posture.title: Studio posture
posture.body: Keep this chapter narrow. The goal is not to feel busy or advanced. The goal is to produce one artifact a sharp operator would still trust tomorrow.

workspace.label: Studio workspace
workspace.chooseStepBody: Choose a step from the chapter spine to start the lane.
workspace.deliverablePlaceholder: Paste the output, transcript note, or artifact that proves this chapter worked.
workspace.benchNotesPlaceholder: Capture the blocker, file path, or command variation that mattered.

reviewState.label: Review state
reviewState.sealedBody: The chapter is sealed. Move on while the route is still fresh.
reviewState.readyBody: The proof exists. Seal the chapter and keep moving.
reviewState.pendingBody: Do not seal the chapter until the proof says something real.

creativeDirection.title: Creative direction
creativeDirection.body: Finish the live step, log one proof artifact, then either seal the chapter or hand off to the next surface.

helpLane.label: Field notes
helpLane.stayHereBody: the current step still contains the answer and the route does not need escalation yet.

surfaceCompass.title: Keep the lesson narrow until the proof is real
surfaceCompass.body: Use Tutor for one grounded blocker, switch to Autopilot when live runtime truth matters, and escalate to Support only after both stop being enough.
surfaceCompass.aside: Recovery belongs below the proof lane, not inside it.

items.askTutorCaption: Use this when one command, file path, or validation check is failing.
items.returnCaption: Step back to the mapped route when the sequence itself is the problem.
items.runtimeCaption: Switch here when the blocker depends on live runtime or approvals.
items.supportCaption: Escalate only after the lesson, tutor, and runtime views stop being enough.

troubleshooting.label: Troubleshooting appendix
troubleshooting.title: The smaller notes you only read when the route goes crooked

labels.chapterBriefTitle: Make one command work and keep the proof
labels.chapterBriefBody: This first chapter should narrow the world fast: install Hermes, run one tiny prompt, and hold on to the output so the first win becomes something real.

fastestPathBody.done: You already cleared the first win. Do not linger here. Open the next lesson and keep the sequence moving.
fastestPathBody.installDone: Good. The install is done. Now run one tiny prompt and get the first visible answer.
fastestPathBody.installFirst: Do not compare providers, frameworks, or stacks yet. Install Hermes and make the command work first.

labels.operatingRuleBody: More choice this early is just prettier procrastination. Get the first win, then widen the surface.
labels.visibleMoves: Three visible moves to clear Chapter 01
labels.liveStackRadarTitle: The onboarding lane now knows what the tracked stacks are actually shipping
labels.liveStackRadarBody: Pico starts with Hermes in the lesson flow, but the product now keeps the rest of the stack map visible instead of pretending there is only one runtime on earth.
labels.missionBoardTitle: Keep the first two missions visibly grounded
labels.missionBoardBody: Onboarding should know where the operator left off. These mission cards mirror the lesson workspace so the first win survives route changes instead of dissolving into memory.
labels.operatorRecordTitle: Keep hosted kickoff and runtime truth in one place
labels.operatorRecordBody: Keep the hosted onboarding state, runtime truth, and next activation move in one operator record for Chapter 01.

activationTrackBody.done: You already got the first win. Use the main action above and keep the sequence moving.
activationTrackBody.installDone: Hermes is installed. Skip the overview and open the first prompt lesson now.
activationTrackBody.installFirst: Everything else can wait. Open the install lesson and get the command working.

labels.operatorRuleBody: More choice this early is still prettier procrastination. Keep the chapter narrow until one local run and one proof artifact are both real.
labels.hostedRuntimeEditorTitle: Update the operator record without leaving the route
labels.hostedRuntimeEditorBody: This does not pretend to install anything from the browser. It stores the latest truthful runtime snapshot so the rest of Pico stops guessing.
labels.saveOnlyTruth: Save only what is true on the operator host right now: gateway URL, runtime health, and the bound assistant.

runtime.signInFirst: Sign in first. The runtime snapshot is per operator account.
runtime.loading: Loading backend onboarding and runtime state.
runtime.noHostedState: No hosted onboarding state has been recorded yet.
runtime.noRuntimeSnapshot: No runtime snapshot has been synced yet.
runtime.noDoctorSummary: No doctor summary was synced yet.

proofStateHeadline.install: Make the first proof impossible to miss.
proofStateHeadline.prompt: Seal the proof while the route is still honest.
proofStateHeadline.cleared: First proof is real. Keep the lane moving.

hero.shellTitle: Get to your first working agent fast
hero.shellDescription: Ignore everything except the first local win. Install Hermes, run one prompt, see a real answer, then keep moving.
hero.subtitle: Track the install, the first proof artifact, and the next move from one place.

doctrine.reviewLine: Do not widen the surface early
doctrine.label: Kickoff doctrine
doctrine.title: Start like a sharp studio, not a hobby project
doctrine.postBody: Keep Chapter 01 narrow on purpose. No browsing, no stack tourism, and no extra setup theater before one real artifact exists.
doctrine.guideMarkerBody: One clear guide cue is enough while the first proof is still forming.

compass.title: Use the shortest route that keeps the product honest
compass.body: Keep the first win narrow. Continue the lesson path when the sequence is clear, use tutor for one blocked command, open Autopilot only when live runtime state is the real question, and escalate last.
compass.aside: This chapter exists to compress the world, not widen it. The first proof should happen before preferences, architecture debates, or tooling tours.
compass.primaryCaption: Stay on the primary sequence until one visible output is real.
compass.tutorCaption: Use this when one concrete command or validation gate is blocking you.
compass.autopilotCaption: Switch here once runtime state, approvals, or spend become the bottleneck.
compass.supportCaption: Only use this when the product path stopped being truthful enough to recover alone.

protocol.body: These are the only moves that count before the surface earns the right to get wider.

shared.askTutorNextStep: Ask tutor for the exact next step
shared.resumeBody: Open the lesson route and keep the proof lane short.
shared.noProofLogged: No proof has been logged yet. Save the single artifact that proves the mission actually worked.

mission.summaryFallback: Step into the live control room when the lesson lane is clear.
mission.validationFallback: Use the runtime when the question is no longer about reading the lesson.

standards.nextStandardFallback: Keep the chapter narrow and honest.
standards.label: Track standards

shell.title: Academy
shell.description: One mission. One proof. One honest next move.

chapterLedger.label: Chapter ledger
chapterLedger.title: One dominant chapter, with the rest of the map kept quiet.
chapterLedger.body: The current track gets the full editorial treatment. The rest of the atlas stays visible, but compressed, so the route still feels authored instead of equally loud everywhere.
chapterLedger.missionCorrectionBody: The atlas stays useful only if it keeps pushing you back into the current mission.

referenceAnnex.label: Reference annex
referenceAnnex.title: Capability, archive, and platform memory stay below the route on purpose
referenceAnnex.body: Platform memory stays available, but it sits under the codex on purpose. The Academy leads with route and proof, then exposes configuration only when you need it.

capabilities.label: Unlocked capabilities
capabilities.body: The pieces that matter after the mission is already real.
capabilities.firstUnlockEmpty: The first capability unlock lands only after the first lessons are cleared for real.

composer.summaryDefault: Outbound send requires a human decision before the runtime crosses the line.
composer.body: Exercise the live approval queue here instead of waiting for another surface to create the request first.
composer.summaryLabel: Why this action needs a gate

runDetail.failedNoMessage: The run stopped without a stored error message.
runDetail.inFlight: The run is still moving through the pipeline.
runDetail.awaitingOwner: The run is paused and waiting for owner input before continuing.
runDetail.completedNoSummary: The run completed without a short summary.

runImpact.failed: This workflow did not finish cleanly. Check the error and traces before trusting the next attempt.
runImpact.active: Work is still in flight. Watch for hangs, retries, or silence that lasts too long.
runImpact.awaitingOwner: The run is paused waiting for owner action. Respond to unblock the pipeline.
runImpact.completed: This run completed. Verify the output is actually useful before you automate it harder.

alertImpact.resolved: The alert is cleared, but you still want the root cause to make sense.
alertImpact.active: This is active operator pain. If you ignore it, the runtime will keep surprising you.

approvalImpact.pending: A risky action is waiting for a human decision. Nothing should proceed past this gate yet.
approvalImpact.approved: The gate opened. Make sure the approved action actually matches the request you intended to allow.
approvalImpact.rejected: The risky action was blocked. Good. Now decide whether the request was wrong or the guardrail is too strict.
approvalImpact.changed: This approval changed state and should be reviewed if it affects live behavior.

error.partialSignals: Some live MUTX signals failed to load. What is shown is partial, not fabricated.
error.loadLiveData: Failed to load live autopilot data
error.createApprovalRequiredFields: agent, session, and action type are required before creating an approval request

noAgent.title: No monitored agent exists yet
noAgent.body: Pico has no real MUTX agent to attach to. Create or deploy one actual agent first, then come back for run history.

noHistory.title: An agent exists, but nothing has run yet
noHistory.body: MUTX knows about at least one agent, but there is no run history yet. Trigger one real task or wait for the first schedule tick, then come back here.

noRuns.title: No alerts because nothing is running yet
noRuns.body: An empty alert feed means nothing until the agent has executed real work. Get one run into MUTX first.

none.title: No live alerts right now
none.body: Good. The monitoring feed is quiet right now. Keep watching the next real run and failure path.

noBudget.title: No live budget snapshot yet
noBudget.body: There is no MUTX budget snapshot to compare against yet. Until that exists, cost awareness is incomplete.

empty.title: Budget exists, but usage is empty
empty.body: The budget surface is live, but no usage events landed in the current window. Either the agent has not spent anything yet or usage emission is missing.

noAgent.title: No agent exists to gate yet
noAgent.body: Approval queues only matter when a real agent is capable of doing something risky. Create or deploy the agent first.

gateOff.title: Approval history exists, but the gate is off locally
gateOff.body: MUTX already has approval records, but Pico still says the gate is disabled. Turn the gate on here so local product state matches the control-plane reality.

none.title: No approval records yet
none.body: Nothing risky has been routed through the real approval queue yet. Exercise one real gated action before you call this governed.

hero.headline: Keep the run, spend, and gate in the same frame.
hero.body: Start with the latest execution, compare it to the current budget line, then decide whether a human gate belongs in the way. That order keeps the control room honest.

nextCheck.label: Next operator check
nextCheck.noRunBody: No run is visible yet, so the honest move is still back on the lesson path.

decisionLine.label: Decision line
decisionLine.thresholdBreached: Spend is already across the line. Decide whether the next risky action needs a human gate.
decisionLine.pendingQueue: The queue is holding risky actions in view. Review them before the surface gets noisier.
decisionLine.clear: If the line is still clear, keep reading the run until the next decision becomes obvious.

read.label: 01 • Read
read.title: Start with the strongest live signal
read.authRequired: Without a hosted session this room should refuse to improvise. Attach the live feed first.
read.withRun: Run {runId} is the first thing to read. {detail}
read.noRun: No live run exists yet. The honest move is to trigger one real task before tuning anything else.

judge.label: 02 • Judge
judge.title: Make the decision line explicit
judge.authRequired: Budget review is unavailable until the live account is attached.
judge.withBudget: {usage} usage against a {threshold} threshold. That line should tell you when a human steps in.
judge.noBudget: No live budget snapshot yet. Do not pretend a threshold matters until it meets real spend.

intervene.label: 03 • Intervene
intervene.title: Keep risky actions reviewable
intervene.pending: {count} approval item{pluralSuffix} is waiting. Good. The dangerous work is still visible.
intervene.configured: The gate is configured, but nothing is waiting right now. Keep it reviewable and boring.
intervene.off: The gate is still off. Turn it on before a risky action becomes an invisible side effect.

operatorDoctrine.title: Read, judge, then intervene
operatorDoctrine.postureBody: Use this room to review live state, not to celebrate movement for its own sake.
operatorDoctrine.decisionLineBody: Start with the last run, compare it against the budget line, then decide whether the gate needs a human call. That order matters.

shell.title: Trust the runtime because the surface tells the truth
shell.description: Inspect the latest run, compare it to live spend, then decide which risky actions deserve a gate. That is how trust gets earned here.

surfaceCompass.title: Stay here only when the live runtime is the real source of truth
surfaceCompass.body: Autopilot is for reading the current run, spend, alerts, and approvals. If the product still needs a lesson answer, go back to academy or tutor. If the runtime truth is visible but still not enough, escalate with the evidence attached.
surfaceCompass.aside: A control room should not ask for faith. If there is no live signal, leave and create one. If the signal exists, use it to decide the next move immediately.

items.lessonCaption: Return here when the real blocker is still on the lesson path rather than in the runtime.
items.askTutor: Ask tutor about the next move
items.askTutorCaption: Use tutor when the product likely still knows the answer and the issue is not live runtime state.
items.stayOnRecentRunsCaption: Remain here when the latest execution, trace, or alert feed is the actual decision surface.
items.escalateCaption: Escalate only after you have the packet, the run context, and the live signal that proves where the truth broke.

banner.authRequired: Live Autopilot needs an authenticated MUTX session. Until then, there is no honest run, alert, budget, or approval feed to show you.

controlBrief.title: Read the runtime before you trust the automation
controlBrief.body: Autopilot earns trust when the last run, the live spend, and the risky actions stay visible in one control surface.

summary.authRequired: Until a MUTX session is attached, this surface refuses to invent truth. Use the academy to finish the setup path, then come back to read the real runtime.
summary.noRun: No live run is visible yet. The next honest move is to finish the academy path, trigger one real task, and return here only once the runtime has something to say.

operatorRail.emptyFeedBody: Do not dress up missing runtime data. Finish the academy path, trigger one real action, and come back only when MUTX has something to report.

runs.label: Runs
runs.noAgent: No agent is connected to MUTX yet.
runs.noRunsYet: An agent exists, but no run has landed yet.
runs.offline: Sign in to read live run history.

failures.label: Failures
failures.hasFailures: Failures are visible here. Good. Hidden failures are worse.
failures.none: No failed runs in the current window.
failures.offline: Sign in to see failed run count.

alerts.label: Alerts
alerts.hasAlerts: Unresolved operator pain from the monitoring feed.
alerts.noneNoRuns: No alerts yet because nothing has executed.
alerts.offline: Sign in to read live alerts.

budget.label: Budget
budget.noSnapshot: No live budget snapshot returned yet.
budget.noUsage: Budget exists, but usage is empty in the current window.
budget.offline: Sign in to read budget usage.

approvals.label: Approvals
approvals.pending: Pending risky actions are waiting for a human call.
approvals.historyGateOff: Approval history exists, but Pico gate is still off locally.
approvals.none: No risky actions are blocked right now.
approvals.offline: Sign in to see live approvals.

sidebar.askTutor: Ask tutor about the next move
sidebar.body: Stay here when the runtime is the source of truth. If the lesson workspace is still incomplete, the academy remains the cleaner route back to a real answer.

controlProtocol.title: Three operator checks before automation earns trust
controlProtocol.body: The control room should stage the first decisions in plain sight. Read the last run, read the spend line, and keep risky actions exposed before you touch anything more abstract.

timeline.title: Read the live story before you touch settings
timeline.body: This is the operator narrative. If the timeline is empty, the correct answer is that nothing meaningful has happened yet.
timeline.authRequired: No timeline without live control-plane access. That is the honest answer.
timeline.olderSignalsHidden: {count} older signal{pluralSuffix} stayed out of view on purpose. Start with the strongest four.

runs.title: Recent execution review
runs.body: Start here. Read the latest run before you touch thresholds or approvals.
runs.authRequired: Sign in, then come back here to inspect the first real run.

card.title: Run {runId}

operatorRead.failed: This job failed. If the agent is still trusted, it should be because you understand this failure.
operatorRead.active: This work is still active. Long silence means you may have a stuck runtime.
operatorRead.finished: The job finished. Now verify the output is useful, not just technically complete.

card.noTraces: No run traces were returned for this run.

runs.olderRunsHidden: {count} older run{pluralSuffix} are hidden so the review stays on the latest decisions first.

spend.title: Live spend and your line in the sand
spend.thresholdBody: Pico stores the local budget line here. Real alert delivery still follows the live MUTX monitoring setup, so this surface refuses to pretend email or webhook routing is active when it is not.
spend.breachedWarning: Threshold breached. Live usage is above the limit you configured.
spend.breakdownAuthRequired: Sign in to read the usage breakdown.

alerts.title: Meaningful monitoring events
alerts.body: This is the live alert feed. No fake warning badges, no synthetic incidents.
alerts.authRequired: Sign in to load live alerts.
alerts.hiddenCount: {count} additional alert{pluralSuffix} are suppressed so the feed stays editorial instead of turning into a wall of badges.

approvals.title: Risky actions and their decisions
approvals.body: This queue is now the approval source of truth for Pico. It should not disappear because a process restarted.
approvals.authRequired: Sign in to load live approval requests.
approvals.gateMismatch: Approval records already exist in MUTX, but Pico still shows the gate as off locally. Turn on the gate here so the product state matches the control-plane reality.
approvals.hiddenPending: {count} more pending approval{pluralSuffix} stayed out of view so the queue keeps the sharpest decisions on top.

recentDecisions.hiddenOlder: {count} older decision{pluralSuffix} are hidden so the recent record stays readable.

authenticated.body: Signed in as {identity}. Progress sync, onboarding state, approvals, and live runtime data can now use the hosted MUTX account attached to this host.
authenticated.verificationNote: Password accounts stay limited until the link sent by email has been confirmed. Finish that before treating the hosted session as production-ready.

productTruth.label: Product truth
productTruth.body: The product can now tell the truth about progress, runtime state, approvals, and hosted configuration. If this account is not production-ready yet, fix that here before trusting the rest of the surface.

loading.label: Hosted session
loading.body: Checking whether an operator session is attached on this Pico host.

anonymous.body: The Pico product routes are live here, but the hosted session is not attached on this domain yet. Sign in within Pico to persist progress, read live runs, and use onboarding/runtime state from the backend. Google, GitHub, Discord, Apple, password sign-up, and email confirmation now all complete on this host.

withoutSession.label: Without session
withoutSession.body: Attach the Pico account on this host before trusting progress, live runtime state, or approvals behavior.

tutor.label: Tutor
tutor.note: Grounded help for the next move

header.label: Platform desk
header.title: Identity, route memory, and limits in one place
header.body: This is the operator-facing control desk for the Pico account. It should tell the truth about plan limits, where the operator last worked, and whether the product surface is tuned for focus or recovery.

routeMemory.label: Route memory
routeMemory.body: The current surface should match what the operator is actually doing. Keep this current when the product changes lanes.

currentSurface.label: Current surface
currentSurface.body: Route memory should follow the operator across Pico instead of resetting at every page change.

lastLessonContext.label: Last lesson context
lastLessonContext.body: This is the recovery point when the operator needs to drop back into the lesson spine.

collapseRail.label: Collapse rail
collapseRail.body: Use a tighter shell when the operator already knows the route and needs more room for the live surface.

keepHelpLaneOpen.label: Keep help lane open
keepHelpLaneOpen.body: Leave recovery guidance visible when the operator is still learning the product routes.

operatorTruth.label: Operator truth
operatorTruth.body: The product should remember route and lesson context without pretending it knows more than it does. `activeSurface`, `lastOpenedLessonSlug`, the rail state, and the help lane are the minimum persisted memory Pico needs to feel like one platform.
